heading: E QUANTI LI HANNO DESCRITTI
card_summary: This long chapter of the Cantico Primo catalogues the dissonance of pagan and secular philosophers in contrast with the perfect consonance of Scripture and Christian theology. Zorzi surveys the cosmological first principles proposed by Leucippus, Democritus, Thales, Anaximenes, Heraclitus, Pythagoras, Socrates, Plato, Aristotle, Epicurus, and Zeno (among many others), then chronicles competing theories of the soul from Anaximander through Plotinus, Proclus, and Averroes, and finishes with the doxography of competing views on the human final end, drawing on Augustine's report that Marcus Varro collected 288 opinions on the subject. Against this cacophony, Zorzi opposes the single harmonious choir of Hebrew prophets, evangelists, apostles, Greek and Latin Church Fathers, and Scholastic theologians — Moses, David, Paul, Augustine, Ambrose, Jerome, Thomas Aquinas, Bonaventure, Duns Scotus — whose diversely-pitched voices produce a divine harmony precisely because they all orient toward the One God as the sole source of consonance. The Hermetic tradition (Hermes Trismegistus, Zoroaster, Orpheus), Hebrew and Chaldean sages, and the Neoplatonists Plato and Plotinus occupy an intermediate position, valued for their partial alignment with divine harmony.
essay:
# E QUANTI LI HANNO DESCRITTI — Expanded Scholarly Essay (pp. 333–365)

## Introduction: Music as Metaphysical Criterion

Zorzi opens this extended chapter with a methodological claim of cardinal importance: harmony (*harmonia*) is not merely an aesthetic or musical concept but a metaphysical principle. Drawing from Boethius—whose *Institutio musica* provided the medieval Latinophone world with its most authoritative account of number, proportion, and cosmic order—Zorzi defines harmony as the coordination of diverse voices or strings (*coordinatio cordae vel vocum*) according to integral rules (*modi integri*) without violation of consonance (*absque violatione concordantiae*). This definition is then weaponized against the entire philosophical tradition of pagan antiquity. Where the philosophers generate what Zorzi calls a *strepitus horribilis*—a horrible noise, an acoustic catastrophe—the tradition rooted in divine revelation produces consonance. The reason, cited from Boethius's formula, is ontological: *omne enim ideo est quia unum numero est*—everything exists insofar as it is one in number. Departure from the One (*ab uno*), who alone confers consonance on all things, necessarily generates irreducible dissonance and multiplicity.

> «Perciò le loro dottrine (del tutto prive di ciò che conferisce a ogni cosa l'essere e la corrispondenza armoniosa), proliferando in moltitudine confusa, sono destinate a dissolversi.»

<cite>p. 333 (Italian translation, chapter 1)</cite>

This principle anchors Zorzi's entire comparative theology. The *De Harmonia Mundi* does not proceed by refuting individual doctrines but by invoking a transcendent criterion—the metaphysical principle of unity-in-multiplicity—that judges all systems according to whether they participate in the divine harmony or fall into cacophony.

---

## Part I: The Internal Dissonance of Philosophical Schools

### The Failure of Philosophical Community

Zorzi begins by cataloguing the divisions within philosophical schools themselves, using these as evidence of philosophy's incapacity to achieve stable truth. The Socratics Aristippus and Ariston, both pupils of Socrates, disagree radically about the *telos* (final end): Aristippus places it in pleasure (*hedone*), Ariston in virtue (*arete*). Aristotle himself, despite being Plato's own student, publicly attacked his teacher's doctrine and covered him with «infami calunnie» (infamous calumnies). The Aristotelian tradition splits into the Alexandrist school (Alexander of Aphrodisias) and the Averroistic school (Ibn Rushd), with irreconcilable differences over the immortality and unity of the intellect. Alexander denies the soul's survival of bodily death; Averroes posits a single universal intellect (*intellectus unus communis*) shared by all humanity, which Zorzi rightly notes evacuates individual rational immortality. Most notably, al-Ghazali attacks all philosophers indiscriminately; Averroes attacks al-Ghazali; and their mutual recriminations are described as a serpent striking back at its own fury.

This opening move is pedagogically shrewd: before Zorzi can argue that pagans have erred about ultimate things, he establishes that they cannot even agree amongst themselves. Internal contradiction is not accidental to pagan philosophy but structural. The Pythagoreans fragment over metempsychosis (transmigration of souls): Jarca the Brahmin and Apollonius of Tyana defend the doctrine; Iamblichus, Porphyry, and Proclus reject it on metaphysical grounds (the soul cannot be uprooted from its *centre*, its proper place in the cosmos); Timaeus of Locri dismisses the entire mythology as pedagogical fright-tales devised to terrorize the vulgar. No consensus emerges even within a school claiming esoteric wisdom.

### The Chaos of Physical Principles (*Arche*)

Zorzi surveys the pre-Socratics' competing answers to the question of the *arche*—the single principle or substrate underlying all generation and change. Thales (c. 636–546 BCE) proposes water, drawing the inference from observation that moisture is present in all animate bodies and that dry matter cannot be alive. Anaximander, Thales's pupil, proposes *apeiron* (the infinite or indefinite), a boundless material substance that transcends any determinate sensible quality. Anaximenes proposes air (*pneuma*), the medium of respiration. Anaxagoras proposes *homeomeria*—infinitely divisible particles of unlike kinds, each containing a speck of everything else. Heraclitus and Hipparchus of Metapontum propose fire (*pyr*), the subtlest of bodies, the very stuff of celestial spheres. Empedocles rejects monism and proposes all four elements together—Jupiter (aether), Pluto (earth), Juno (air), and Nestis (water)—identified in poetic language as principles in eternal cyclical strife and harmony.

Zorzi notes that Epicurus, building on the atomism of Leucippus and Democritus, adds to the materialist scheme the property of *pondus* (weight)—atoms are not merely shaped (*figura*) and sized (*magnitudo*) but possess an inherent downward tendency that explains their collisions and interactions. Yet all these schemes share a fateful blindness: none of them explains whether matter was created by God, whether matter is itself eternal and co-creative with divinity, or whether it is an uncreated *spouse* of some cosmological principle (as the poets imagined Demogorgon, father of the gods). This lacuna—the refusal or inability to ground matter itself in a theological principle—is Zorzi's decisive indictment.

### The Neoplatonic Exception: Pythagorean Number

Zorzi interrupts the catalog to praise Pythagoras for ascending higher than most suppose. Rather than positing a material substance, Pythagoras placed the principles in formal or natural numbers—what Zorzi calls *numeri formales vel naturales*, not the *mercantile numbers* (*numeri quo utuntur mercatores*) used by traders, but the numbers known only to those who have learned to philosophize and theologize through number.

> «Pitagora, spintosi a profondità maggiori di quanto credano alcuni, ritenne che i princìpi delle cose fossero creati dai numeri e dalle misure, ovvero le opportune proporzioni delle realtà soggiacenti (tali proporzioni sono dette armonie, accordi e concordanze); non ci si riferisce certo ai numeri dei quali si servono i mercanti, ma ai numeri formali o naturali.»

<cite>p. 334 (chapter 3, Italian text)</cite>

This passage opens onto one of the deepest layers of Zorzi's thought. For Pythagoras and his successors, *number* is not a mere abstraction or counting device but the very structure of reality. The *harmonies* (*harmoniai*), *concords* (*concordantiae*), and *proportions* (*proportiones*) through which diverse elements accord are themselves numerical relationships. Later in the text, Zorzi will develop a fourfold hierarchy of number: *vocale* (number as spoken or named), *formale* (number as the intelligible structure of form), *razionale* (number as grasped by discursive reason), and *divino* (number as the divine emanation from the One). Those who failed to grasp this hierarchy—who did not understand that one could *ascend* from spoken number through formal number to the divine number—fell into confusion and multiplied incompatible doctrines.

Alcmaeon of Croton, following Pythagoras, posits unity as the effective principle and the infinite dyad as the material, passive substrate and source of all multiplicity. Anaximander, advancing further, takes the infinite itself (*apeiron*, what the Latins call *interminata materia*) as the subjective principle precisely because it lacks determinate nature, form, and quantity—and thus serves as the indefinite ground for infinite cycles of world-generation and world-dissolution.

---

## Part II: The Choir of Sacred Writers and the Celestial Hierarchy

### The Harmony of Unified Voices

Against the philosophers' babel, Zorzi sets an extraordinary vision of theological consonance. The controlling analogy is the ancient musical *systema*: the Greek musical scale with its named degrees—hypate (highest or deepest, depending on system), parhypate, lichanos, mese (middle string), paramese, and the succeeding strings. If all strings sounded the same pitch, no harmony would result; instead, the diversity of tones (grave and acute, low and high) coordinated by numerical proportion (*proportio*) produces beauty.

The sacred writers—Moses, Job, David, Solomon, all the prophets, the evangelists, Paul and the apostolic company—together with superhuman intelligences (the angelic choirs), sing in perfect consonance not because they all voice identical truths but because they all orient toward the *One God*, the single source from which true wisdom emanates. Zorzi employs the doctrine of celestial hierarchy inherited from Pseudo-Dionysius. John and Augustine ring out as seraphim (the highest, most incandescent angels, burning with divine love); Matthew and Ambrose emit grave sentential voices among the thrones; Luke and Gregory delight in cherubic sweetness; Jerome and Mark crush heretical cacophony with the sterner voice of the powers and virtues; Solomon and Bernard intone the epithalamic songs of the celestial marriage of Bride and Bridegroom.

Each Greek and Latin Father and Scholastic is then mapped onto this hierarchy. Duns Scotus and Francis of Assisi correspond to Augustine but with different temperament—Scotus with graver, more intense movement (*tardiori motu*); Francis with swifter, more acute resonance. Albert the Great, with his dense investigation of the secrets of both nature and supernature (*secretorum naturae utriusque*), emulates Ambrose. Thomas Aquinas—"the Angelic Doctor"—follows Bernard in sweetness and Augustine in magnificence, achieving a synthesis of devotional and doctrinal music. Bonaventure sings with Gregory in a modulation where devotion predominates over doctrine (*devota magis quam docta modulatione*). Giles of Rome and Peter Aureoli recall Jerome's Roman eloquence; Henry of Ghent, at the level of Origen, refreshes elevated minds with elevated doctrine.

### The Pagan Philosophers as Discordant Animals

By contrast, the pagan philosophers are likened to different beasts—some bellowing (*mugitum*), others neighing (*hinnitum*), bleating (*balatum*), roaring (*rugitum*), barking (*latratum*), or braying (*ruditum*)—producing an impossible cacophony. The image is not merely insulting but theologically functional: where the sacred writers participate in divine unity and thus achieve harmony despite diversity, the philosophers have severed themselves from the source of consonance and thus remain imprisoned in multiplicity and contradiction.

---

## Part III: The Intermediate Traditions—Hermeticism, Orphism, and Neoplatonism

Zorzi situates several figures and traditions in an intermediate position—neither the dissonance of materialist philosophy nor the full consonance of Christian revelation, but capable of partial alignment with divine harmony.

### Hermes Trismegistus and Egyptian Wisdom

Hermes Trismegistus (whether understood as Egyptian by common opinion or Midianite by others) is praised for having achieved unity with his own intellect (*cum sua mente unitus*), thereby transcending human discourse and always exhaling what is divine. The historical Hermes is here understood through the lens of the *Hermetica*—late antique texts attributed to Hermes and influential in Renaissance Neoplatonism. Zorzi credits him with touching genuine truths about the divine and natural marriage (*epithalamia*) through which the cosmos is ordered.

### Pythagoreanism and Mystery

Iamblichus, devoted to numbers and mysteries, strives to transmit the Pythagorean and Egyptian sacraments. Iamblichus (245–325 CE) represents the theurgical turn in late Neoplatonism—a movement seeking not merely intellectual illumination but ritual communion with divine principles. His emphasis on *mysterion* and sacred rite aligns with Zorzi's conviction that divine truth is often concealed under veils that require initiation to penetrate.

### Orphism, Zoroastrianism, and Natural Magic

Zoroaster, Orpheus, and Charondas the Persian penetrate the secrets of nature and sound the sweet wedding-songs of the natural marriage of the world. The *Orphica* represent for Renaissance thinkers a parallel sacred tradition to Scripture—a pre-Christian revelation of divine order. Zoroastrianism, with its dualism of light and darkness, its ethics of cosmic order (*asha*), and its angelology, held obvious appeal to Christian theologians seeking correlates to Christian cosmology. These traditions are not simply concordant with Christianity but represent *partial* consonance—they gesture toward truths they do not fully possess.

### Hebrew and Chaldean Sages

The Hebrew and Chaldean sages (*sapientes Hebraeorum et Chaldaeorum*), instructed by divine oracles, sing a more perfect marriage—that of the archetypal pattern of the cosmic machine (*archetypo*) with the human world. This is crucial: Zorzi grades the traditions hierarchically. The pagan philosophers are wholly lost; the Hermeticists and Orphic poets possess partial insight; but the Hebrew and Chaldean sages, rooted in revealed religion, achieve a consonance approaching that of Christian theology. The "Chaldean" reference likely encompasses both Jewish and Islamic philosophy (the Sabian tradition was understood as Chaldean). In any case, revelation is the distinguishing mark: those instructed by divine oracles (*divinis oraculis*) achieve a higher harmony than those relying on human reason alone.

---

## Part IV: The Soul in Philosophical Discord

The section on *animae natura* (the nature of the soul) represents the longest and most intricate doxography. Zorzi opens with Aristotle's own confession that knowledge of the soul is among the most difficult (*censeat difficillimorum*), supported by Lucretius: *Ignorant multi, quae sit natura animae*—many do not know what the soul's nature is.

### Materialist and Dynamist Theories

Anaximander proposes that man evolved from fish-like creatures in heated earth. Thales posits matter akin to fire combined with earth. The Pythagoreans divide: some identify the soul with motes in the air (*minuta quae sunt in aere*), others with the force moving those motes (*ea movens*). Dicaearchus defines the soul as a self-moving body. Anaximenes says air; Xenophanes, earth and water; Parmenides and Boetus, earth and fire. Epicurus compounds fire, air, and vital spirit in atomic sympathy (*pro atomorum similitudine*)—a single displaced atom moves the whole soul, which is why the sight of snow recalls simultaneously whiteness and cold, or why yawning spreads contagiously through a group.

Zorzi traces Epicurus's doctrine to ancient Arab philosophers called *Maurasphes*, who admitted the spirit's entry (*ingressum*) into the soul but not its exit (*egressum*), holding that it dissolves into nothing. The *Maurasphes* identification is obscure—possibly a corruption of a name—but the reference underscores Zorzi's ambitious attempt to integrate Islamic and pre-Islamic philosophy into his comparative scheme.

### The Location and Composition Debates

Diogenes the Cynic, Empedocles, Democritus and Leucippus, Crizia, Hippocrates of Cos, Asclepiades, Zeno (the Stoic), Galen, Hipparchus, Heraclitus of Ephesus, Heraclides Ponticus, Critolaus, Posidonius, Chrysippus—each proposes a different seat or substance. Hippocrates lodges the soul's principal power in the head; Epicurus counters that decapitated animals continue to live momentarily. Empedocles places it in the heart; Epicurus again objects that crocodiles survive and resist violence even after their hearts are removed. The Stoics, with Chrysippus, place the soul (*hegemonikon*) in the heart, vehicled by *pneuma* (breath/spirit) rather than blood as Empedocles claimed. Others follow Moses in locating the soul in the blood, since the Hebrew scriptures forbid the consumption of blood because the animal's soul (*nephesh*) resides in it.

This entire catalog, Zorzi notes, remains *sub iudice*—under judicial deliberation, unresolved even in his own time. The very persistence of the disagreement witnesses to philosophy's failure to achieve stable knowledge of the soul's nature.

### Aristotle's Evasion and Its Consequences

Zorzi reserves particularly sharp criticism for Aristotle's definition of the soul as the *entelecheia* (perfection or actuality) of an organic body possessing life potentially. This definition, he argues, describes the *effect* rather than the *essence* or *origin* of the soul—comparable to defining the Pope or Emperor by saying he commands armies. Aristotle's deliberate obscurity on the soul's duration and immortality (*circa naturam eius et durationem adeo sese continuit*) generated the fatal bifurcation into Alexandrist mortalism and Averroistic universal-intellect doctrine, both of which Zorzi judges utterly remote from piety and truth.

The implicit critique is that Aristotle, by refusing to offer a metaphysical account of the soul's substantial nature and divine origin, created a vacuum into which each successor poured his own speculation. Plotinus offers a more sophisticated account, but even he occupies an intermediate position.

### Pythagoras and Plato: Veiled Wisdom

Zorzi treats Pythagoras and Plato with considerable respect, even as he notes their reticence. Pythagoras held that the soul is a divine *genus* (*divinum quoddam genus*) lodged in human beings, full of forms but submerged and nearly suffocated by the corporeal forest (*corporea sylva*), as if it had drunk the waters of Lethe—the river of forgetfulness. This image of the soul's *fall* into matter, so common in Neoplatonism, resonates with Christian accounts of the soul's descent into flesh. Timaeus of Locri and Plato imitated this teaching through the veil of Pythagorean mystery.

Zorzi defends their practice of concealment, citing Plato's own admission in *De Legibus* X:

> «Qualis (inquit) anima sit, et quam habeat vim omnes ferme ignorasse videntur, si quid tamen de ipsa tradiderunt, per numeros tamquam sibi magis cognitos processere.»

<cite>p. 182 (chapter 4, Italian text)</cite>

Almost everyone remains ignorant of what the soul truly is; those ancient sages who transmitted anything about it proceeded through *numbers*, as if numbers were more knowable to them. This is the crucial point: Plato and Pythagoras concealed soul-doctrine under numerical symbolism not from mere prudence but from metaphysical insight—the soul's nature is apprehended through the ladder of numbers.

### The Fourfold Hierarchy of Number

Here Zorzi introduces a cardinal teaching: there are four orders of number. Those who failed to grasp this hierarchy—*numero vocale*, *formale*, *razionale*, *divino*—descended into delirium (*deliramentum*). The *numero vocale* is number as named or spoken. The *numero formale* is number as the intelligible form or structure. The *numero razionale* is number as grasped by discursive reason (*ratio*). The *numero divino* is number as it emanates from the divine unity. One ascends from the spoken to the formal to the rational to the divine; those who do not understand this fourfold progression, or who proceed "with longer strides and devious paths," scatter into various byways.

Another passes among later Neoplatonists: Anaxander equates the soul with number; Aristoxenus, with a number that moves itself. Severus grants the soul geometric essence composed of point (indivisible) and distance (divisible). Plutarch and Atticus distinguish the irrational divisible part from the divine indivisible part, the former serving as substrate, the latter as form and emanation. Plotinus places the soul between intellect and sensation, individual in respect of intellect but divisible in the bodies it animates. Amelius and Theodorus ascend higher still, positing two intellects before the soul—one containing universal ideas, the other particular ideas—with the soul intermediate.

Proclus holds the soul to be both one and many, individual and multiplied according to the number of subjects, following (but not fully explicating) Plato's teaching, which Plato himself refused to publish, writing to Dionysius that divine mysteries are not to be divulged.

---

## Part V: The Final End in Philosophical Multiplicity

Zorzi notes that Augustine, drawing on Marcus Varro, collected no fewer than **288 different opinions** (*ducentos octoginta octo opiniones*) regarding the final end (*finis*) of human life. Plotinus compiled eight views in his treatise *On the One and the Good* (*Enneads* I.7). Rather than exhaustively catalog all such views, Zorzi surveys the most frequent schools.

The Stoics (Zeno, Cleanthes, Posidonius, Hecaton, Dionysius of Babylon) place the end in laborious virtue (*virtutes laboriosae*), though Antisthenes specifies virtue of soul alone. The Epicureans (Aristippus, Eudoxus of Cnidus, the Cyrenaics) place it in pleasures antithetical to labor. The Peripatetics place it in *contemplation* (*speculatio*). The Platonists, "always reeking of divine decrees" (*semper divina redolentes decreta*), place it in union with the supreme Good.

Zorzi treats the Platonic position as most consonant with Christian doctrine, for it orients the soul toward divine communion rather than toward pleasure or mere intellectual satisfaction. He cites Plotinus's teaching that the soul must recover the wings of twofold philosophy—ascending through *virtue* from the dark forest toward Mind, and through *wisdom* toward God. The two-fold ascent (ethical and contemplative) aligns with the Christian doctrine of charity and knowledge.

---

## Conclusion: The Metaphysical Foundation of Harmony

This chapter's architecture is masterfully conceived. By exhaustively cataloging philosophical dissonance, Zorzi establishes that departure from the *One*—the principle itself—necessarily generates multiplicity and contradiction. Only those traditions rooted in divine revelation, oriented toward God as the sole source of being and consonance, achieve the stable unity of voice-in-diversity that marks genuine harmony. The sacred writers produce harmony not by all repeating the same doctrine but by all converging on the One God as their measure and source.

The gradation of traditions—from utter pagan confusion through Hermetic and Neoplatonic partial insight to Hebrew revelation and finally Christian fulfillment—structures Zorzi's entire theological project. Each level contains truth proportional to its proximity to the source of truth. And the numerical symbolism, the fourfold hierarchy, the musical analogies—all serve to demonstrate that harmony is not arbitrary preference but the objective structure of reality itself, knowable to those who learn to "theologize through number."

Zorzi thus weaponizes the entire history of Western philosophy against itself: philosophy, by failing to acknowledge the One as the ground of all consonance, generates the very cacophony it cannot resolve. The cure is not better philosophy but revelation—the restoration of humanity's orientation toward the divine source that alone confers being, unity, and harmony on all things.
